1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.
61	
62	\begin{definition}[Identity component of a group scheme]
63	  \label{def:identity_component_group_scheme}
64	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
65	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps;
66	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
67	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2851-L2863).
68	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
69	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lem:agps}
70	  %  Let \(k\) be a field, and \(G\) a group scheme locally of finite
71	  % type.  Let \(G^0\) denote the connected component of the identity element \(e\).
72	  %
73	  % \tu{(1)} Then \(G\) is separated.
74	  %
75	  % \tu{(2)} Then \(G\) is smooth if it has a geometrically
76	  % reduced open subscheme.
77	  %
78	  %  \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
79	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
80	  % extending \(k\).
81	  %  \end{lem}"
82	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps.}
83	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type
84	  whose identity section \(e : \Spec k \to G\) factors through \(G\). The
85	  \emph{identity component} of \(G\), denoted \(G^0\), is the locally closed
86	  subscheme of \(G\) whose underlying topological space is the connected
87	  component of \(|G|\) containing the image of \(e\), equipped with the
88	  reduced-induced (a priori) subscheme structure inherited from \(G\).
89	  In characteristic zero, or more generally when \(G\) has a
90	  geometrically reduced open subscheme, the substructure agrees with
91	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.
92	\end{definition}
93	
94	\begin{theorem}[Identity component is an open subgroup scheme of finite type]
95	  \label{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
96	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
97	  
98	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);
99	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
100	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2860-L2863).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
102	  % " \tu{(3)} Then \(G^0\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite
103	  % type; it is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with
104	  % extending \(k\)."
105	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3).}
106	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(G\) a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
107	  type. Then the identity component \(G^0\)
108	  (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is an open and closed
109	  subscheme of \(G\), the open immersion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a
110	  homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes (in particular \(G^0\) inherits a
111	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\)), \(G^0\) is of finite type over
112	  \(k\) and geometrically irreducible, and the formation of \(G^0\)
113	  commutes with extension of the base field: for any field extension
114	  \(K/k\), the natural map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is an isomorphism.
115	\end{theorem}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
118	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
119	% (3).)
120	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
121	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
122	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
123	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
124	% \(G^0\) is open too.
125	%
126	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
127	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
128	% extending \(k\).
129	%
130	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
131	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
132	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
133	%
134	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
135	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
136	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
137	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
138	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
139	% [\dots]
140	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
141	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
142	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
143	% [\dots]
144	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
145	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
146	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
147	\begin{proof}
148	  
149	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
150	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
151	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
152	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
153	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
154	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
155	  the open-subscheme structure.
156	
157	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
158	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
159	  particular the formation of \(G^0\) commutes with extension of the
160	  base field. The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is again connected
161	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
162	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
163	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
164	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group operation
165	  (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
166	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes.
167	
168	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
169	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
170	  commutes with field extension). Over \(\overline k\), the product
171	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
172	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
173	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
174	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
175	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
176	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
177	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
178	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
179	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
180	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
181	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
182	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
183	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
184	\end{proof}
185	
186	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
187	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
188	
189	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to

229	\section{The degree map}
230	\label{sec:pic_degree}
231	
232	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
233	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
234	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
235	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
236	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
237	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
238	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
239	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
240	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
241	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
242	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
243	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
244	degree.
245	
246	\begin{definition}[Degree of a Picard class]
247	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
248	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
249	  
250	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
251	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
252	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
253	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
254	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
255	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
256	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
257	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
258	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
259	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
260	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
261	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
262	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
263	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
264	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
265	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
266	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
267	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
268	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
269	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
270	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
271	  \[
272	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
273	  \]
274	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
275	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
276	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
277	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
278	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
279	  \[
280	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
281	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
282	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
283	  \]
284	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
285	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
286	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
287	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
288	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
289	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
290	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
291	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
292	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
293	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
294	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
295	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
296	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
297	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
298	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
299	  of degree \(d\).
300	\end{definition}

302	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
303	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
304	
305	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
306	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
307	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
308	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
309	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
310	automatically.
311	
312	\begin{theorem}[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety of dimension \(g\)]
313	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
314	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
315	  
316	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
317	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
318	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
319	  % L3990-L3996).
320	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
321	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
322	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
323	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
324	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
325	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
326	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
327	  % \end{ex}"
328	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
329	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
330	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
331	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
332	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
333	  % Exercise~REF."
334	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
335	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
336	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8) and Rmk.~III.1.4(e),
337	  p.~86 (dimension equals genus).}
338	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
339	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). Then the identity component
340	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an
341	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)}: a smooth, proper, geometrically
342	  irreducible commutative \(k\)-group scheme. Its dimension is
343	  \[
344	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g \;=\; g(C),
345	  \]
346	  the genus of \(C\) (\cref{def:genus}). Its \(k\)-points are exactly the
347	  classes of invertible sheaves of degree zero:
348	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\), the kernel of the degree map of
349	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.
350	\end{theorem}
351	
352	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
353	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
354	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
355	% identity-component substrate.)
356	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
357	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
358	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
359	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
360	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
361	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
362	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
363	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
364	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
365	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
366	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
367	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
368	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
369	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
370	% everywhere."
371	% (from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86:)
372	% "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
373	% then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
374	% when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
375	% \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
376	% from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
377	\begin{proof}
378	  
379	  We assemble the conclusion from four inputs.
380	
381	  \emph{Open and closed subgroup of finite type, geometrically
382	  irreducible.} By
383	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
384	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
385	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
386	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
387	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
388	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
389	  separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
390	  and connected: it is a complete subscheme \emph{candidate} for the
391	  abelian-variety conclusion.
392	
393	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
394	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
395	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
396	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
397	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
398	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
399	
400	  \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper geometrically
401	  integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is also geometrically
402	  normal (the smoothness implies geometric regularity, hence geometric
403	  normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p then upgrades the
404	  quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
405	  projective over \(k\), in particular proper. The proof of th:qpp\&p
406	  uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling
407	  out non-constant maps \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a
408	  geometrically normal \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
409	
410	  \emph{Smoothness and dimension.} By Kleiman~\S 5, Cor.~cor:sm,
411	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g\)
412	  (\cref{def:genus}), with equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is
413	  smooth at \(0\), in which case it is smooth of dimension \(g\)
414	  everywhere. For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the Picard scheme is
415	  smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of Kleiman~\S 5, which
416	  asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat with integral geometric
417	  fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the generalised Jacobians
418	  \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative
419	  dimension \(p_a\); specialising to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth
420	  proper curve of (geometric = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives
421	  smoothness and dimension \(g\) for \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
422	
423	  Combining all four steps: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
424	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\). A
425	  proper connected \(k\)-group variety is by definition an
426	  \emph{abelian variety} (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically
427	  commutative (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an
428	  abelian variety of dimension \(g\).
429	
430	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map.} A class
431	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of the
432	  identity precisely when the Hilbert polynomial of a representing
433	  invertible sheaf coincides with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\) ---
434	  equivalently, when its degree (the leading coefficient of the
435	  Hilbert polynomial of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
436	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb Z)\).
437	\end{proof}

493	\section{Out of scope}
494	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
495	
496	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
497	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
498	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
499	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
500	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
501	
502	\begin{itemize}
503	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
504	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
505	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
506	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
507	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
508	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6) --- not needed for the Jacobian's
509	    existence as an abelian variety, hence out of scope.
510	  \item \textbf{The torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
511	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
512	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
513	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
514	    Route~A.
515	  \item \textbf{Construction of the degree map as a morphism of group
516	    schemes \(\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\).} This chapter
517	    defines the degree on \(k\)-points only; the upgrade to a
518	    morphism of group schemes (with the constant group scheme
519	    \(\underline{\mathbb{Z}}_k\)) is the natural assembly statement but
520	    is not required for any downstream consumer in this project.
521	\end{itemize}
